title.en: Capabilities & Benchmarks: Reading Past the Leaderboard

description.en: Learn what public benchmarks actually measure, why leaderboard rank rarely predicts your workload's success, and how to build a weighted, eval-driven view of model capability instead.

story_variants.en:
## The Leaderboard Trap

Every model launch comes with a chart: our bars taller than their bars. Claude, GPT and Gemini releases all do it, and the numbers are usually technically true. Yet enterprises that pick a vendor off a launch chart are routinely surprised three months later. Why? Because **benchmarks measure benchmark performance** — and your workload is not a benchmark.

This lesson is about becoming the person in the room who can read past the leaderboard.

## What the Common Benchmarks Actually Test

Public benchmarks cluster into a few families, each with known blind spots:

- **Knowledge and reasoning exams** (multi-subject question sets, graduate-level science quizzes): good proxies for raw knowledge and short-form reasoning; weak proxies for long, messy, multi-turn work.
- **Coding benchmarks** (function-completion suites, resolve-this-real-GitHub-issue evaluations): the issue-resolution style is one of the more predictive public signals for agentic coding, but scores vary with the scaffolding used — the harness matters as much as the model.
- **Math and competition-style tests**: showcase reasoning-model gains (OpenAI's o-series, extended-thinking modes in Claude, Gemini's thinking variants) but saturate quickly and reward chain-of-thought length.
- **Human-preference arenas** (crowd-voted head-to-head chats): capture *style* and likability as much as correctness; a model can climb by being confident and well-formatted.
- **Long-context tests** (find facts planted deep in huge inputs): early versions were nearly meaningless because retrieval-style lookup is easy; harder variants that require *reasoning across* the whole context are more honest.

> **Callout:** Contamination is real. Benchmark questions leak into training data, and vendors — all of them — have strong incentives to optimize for headline numbers. Treat every self-reported score as marketing until reproduced independently.

## Capability Axes That Matter to Enterprises

Instead of one score, evaluate models along axes weighted by *your* workload:

| Axis | What to probe | Who tends to shine |
|---|---|---|
| Deep reasoning | Multi-step analysis, planning | Flagship/reasoning tiers of all three |
| Agentic coding | Multi-file edits, tool loops | Claude has a strong reputation; others compete hard |
| Instruction following | Format compliance, constraints | Varies by generation — test, don't assume |
| Long context | Synthesis across huge inputs | Gemini's headline strength |
| Multimodality | Images, audio, video in | Gemini native; Claude/GPT strong on vision |
| Latency & throughput | Time-to-first-token, tokens/sec | Economy tiers; varies by region and load |
| Safety behavior | Refusals, jailbreak resistance | Claude's heritage; all three invest heavily |

No vendor wins every row, and rankings shuffle with each generation. The table you should trust is the one your own evals produce.

## Reasoning Modes Changed the Game

Since the o-series and the extended-thinking variants of Claude and Gemini arrived, capability is no longer a fixed property of a model — it is a *dial*. The same model can answer instantly or spend thousands of "thinking" tokens before responding. Consequences:

- Benchmark scores now depend on the **thinking budget** used, which launch charts don't always disclose prominently.
- Cost and latency comparisons must specify the reasoning setting, or they are meaningless.
- For your workload, the right question becomes: *how much thinking does this task need?* Paying for deep reasoning on a sentiment tag is waste; skipping it on contract analysis is risk.

## Build Your Own Scorecard

The professional move is a **weighted scorecard** driven by a private eval set:

1. Collect 50-200 real examples of your task, with gold answers or a grading rubric.
2. Define capability axes and weight them by business impact (reasoning 50%, speed 20%, cost 30% — whatever is true for you).
3. Run every candidate model through the same harness; grade with humans, rubrics, or an LLM-judge you have sanity-checked.
4. Compute weighted scores, rank, and record the date and model versions — results expire.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Teams run one vibe-check prompt, get a good answer, and declare a winner. Single anecdotes have enormous variance. Equally bad: outsourcing judgment entirely to an LLM-as-judge without ever auditing whether the judge agrees with your experts.

A scorecard also depoliticizes vendor debates. When the CTO loves Vendor A and the engineers love Vendor B, "let's run the eval" beats every opinion in the room.

## Reading a Model Launch Like a Pro

When the next Claude, GPT or Gemini generation drops, run this checklist: Which benchmarks improved, and are they *your* kind of task? Was the reasoning budget disclosed? Are the comparisons against the competitor's current flagship or last year's? What changed in price and context window — capability per dollar often matters more than capability alone? And finally: has anything independent (third-party evals, arena results a few weeks in) confirmed the story?

In the practice challenge you will implement the heart of the scorecard: a weighted capability scorer that ranks three mock models against workload-specific weights — deterministic, auditable, and exactly what your future eval pipeline will do at scale.

Leaderboards are a fine place to start a shortlist. They are a terrible place to end a decision.

description.en: Benchmarks feed a shortlist; your private evals and weights make the decision.

left.label: Pick the vendor with the tallest launch-chart bar

right.label: Rank models with a weighted scorecard on your own eval set

description.en: Launch charts are marketing-true; workload evals are decision-true.

explanation.en: Capability ratings come from your eval harness — mock 1-5 values here.
Weights encode business priorities; a latency-critical chatbot weights speed very differently from a contracts analyzer.
The weighted sum collapses many axes into one comparable, auditable number.
Round consistently so rankings are reproducible run-to-run.

description.en: A deterministic ranking: the 'weakest' model on raw reasoning wins once speed and cost are weighted in.
The eval loop that replaces leaderboard-driven decisions — rerun it every model generation.

instruction.en: Implement the heart of a model-selection scorecard: a weighted capability scorer. Complete weighted_score(caps, weights) to return the sum of caps[axis] * weights[axis] over every axis in weights, rounded to 1 decimal place. Then rank the three mock models in MODELS from highest to lowest score and print EXACTLY four lines: one '<model>: <score>' line per model in descending score order, then 'winner=<top model>'. This is exactly what an eval pipeline does after grading: collapse per-axis results into one auditable ranking.

en: The scorer is one line: return round(sum(caps[k] * weights[k] for k in weights), 1).
Build a list of (name, score) pairs, then sort with sorted(pairs, key=lambda p: p[1], reverse=True).
Print each pair as f"{name}: {score}" — round(4.0, 1) prints as 4.0, which is what the expected output shows.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert weighted_score({"reasoning": 5, "speed": 2, "cost_efficiency": 2}, WEIGHTS) == 3.5
assert weighted_score({"reasoning": 4, "speed": 4, "cost_efficiency": 3}, WEIGHTS) == 3.7
assert weighted_score({"reasoning": 3, "speed": 5, "cost_efficiency": 5}, WEIGHTS) == 4.0
assert weighted_score({"reasoning": 0, "speed": 0, "cost_efficiency": 0}, WEIGHTS) == 0.0

q.en: Why is a high public-leaderboard rank a weak predictor of success on your enterprise workload?

en: Benchmarks measure benchmark-style tasks, which rarely match your real data, formats and failure costs
Leaderboards are updated too frequently to be useful
Mixing latency and quality metrics in one chart
Benchmark questions leaking into training data, inflating scores
Using the wrong tokenizer during evaluation

q.en: Since reasoning models (o-series, extended thinking) arrived, why must comparisons state the thinking budget?

en: Thinking budgets are fixed per vendor
Capability, cost and latency all change with how much inference-time 'thinking' the model is allowed
Thinking tokens are free, so budgets don't matter

q.en: In a weighted scorecard, why can a model with the lowest raw reasoning rating still win?

en: Because reasoning is never important in enterprises
Because workload weights on speed and cost efficiency can outweigh its reasoning deficit
Because scorecards ignore reasoning by design
